docs.title: Research Chatlogs
docs.text: This site hosts sanitized analysis summaries from the FlashAttention-4 white-box analytical model research topic.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Raw conversational wire logs are excluded from the public release. Only high-level analysis summaries are published here.

docs.title: Chat Log Analysis
docs.text:
This directory contains extracted timelines from the Kimi Code sessions copied into ../raw/. The primary reading surface is merged-timeline.md, which merges all direct user-level events across sessions in chronological order.

Each timeline keeps only direct user-level events from agents/main/wire.jsonl: typed user prompts, user-slash skill activations, cancel/interruption events, and the final visible assistant response for the matched turn when Kimi recorded one.

Hidden thinking, tool calls, tool results, injected reminders, background task notifications, model-invoked skill calls, and subagent-internal conversations are intentionally omitted from these analysis files. The full event streams remain available under ../raw/.

docs.title: Files
docs.text: File Purpose merged-timeline.md Single chronological timeline across all copied sessions. Event headings use Event <index> - <Short Title>, followed by a blockquoted Time and Session reference.

docs.title: Extraction Notes
docs.text:
• User Prompt: comes from context.append_message records with message.role == "user" and message.origin.kind == "user".
  • User Action: includes user-slash skill activations from message.origin.kind == "skill_activation" with trigger == "user-slash", plus cancel/interruption records from turn.cancel. Model-invoked skill calls are internal AI actions and are omitted from this direct-user timeline.
  • AI: is the text from visible content.part events in the matched turn whose step ended with finishReason == "end_turn". If the turn never reached end_turn, the file says so and shows any visible assistant text that was recorded before the turn stopped or continued.
  • Matching first checks whether the user event occurred during an active main-agent turn, which captures user steers and interrupts. Otherwise it uses the next nearby main-agent turn. Cancellation events remain standalone actions.

docs.title: Confusion Analysis
docs.text: This note summarizes what the flash-attention-4-whitebox-runtime-model chatlog analysis shows about patterns that misled the agent and required explicit user correction. The main source is merged-timeline.md in this directory.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
The recurring pattern is that the agent often accepts a nearby proxy for success: emulator accuracy instead of real B200 validation, coarse NCU bottleneck labels instead of component/path proof, build success instead of rendered-paper inspection, and a failed run instead of experiment evidence. The sections below organize those corrections by the three work surfaces the user kept sharpening: analytical-model definition, experimentation, and paper writing.

proxy success -> agent declares progress -> user tightens reality check
     |                    |                           |
     |                    v                           v
emulator MAPE       "fixed" PDF              real B200 / page image /
coarse NCU label    generated artifact       exact hardware component

docs.title: Correction Map
docs.text: Misleading cue Agent tendency User correction Emulator has ground truth Report accuracy-like confidence Require real B200 validation for accuracy claims NCU says compute-bound Treat bottleneck as explained Ask for exact saturated component and blocking path Build command finishes Assume PDF or figure is fixed Inspect rendered page and preserve semantics Run fails or is slow Treat experiment as infeasible Diagnose the measurement path before rejecting the experiment Simulator repo exists Focus on running simulator Use simulator as architecture/modeling reference Formula works in code Use code identifiers in paper math Use concise symbols and define notation near use

docs.title: User Prompts That Built the Analytical Model
docs.text: The chatlog also shows a separate, constructive thread: the user repeatedly refined what the analytical model was supposed to be. This matters because many corrections were not isolated fixes; they were attempts to pull the agent toward a sharper model contract.

docs.title: Core Model Contract
docs.text:
The initial topic prompt in Event 002 defines the core model: a white-box Flash Attention 4 runtime model that predicts milliseconds from input, follows the GPU internal execution model, avoids complex black-box fitting, identifies bottlenecks, and bases analysis on hardware rather than per-input kernel execution.

Events 004, 006, and 007 make that contract concrete. The user adds NCU because the model must reach SM/cache level, sets NVIDIA B200 as the target GPU, and requires B200 architecture, execution flow, hardware specifics, closed-form formulas for each model part, probabilistic handling for random effects such as cache misses, and prior literature/source study on GPU runtime modeling.

Event 012 then clarifies the validation target: the model should compare measured versus predicted results, and some stages should predict NCU counter trends, not only end-to-end runtime.

docs.title: Real-Hardware Validation Contract
docs.text:
Events 026 through 029 define the evidence bar. The user asks for theoretical model output versus actual runs, challenges emulator-only error with “without actually run something on b200, how do you know the error,” tightens the topic requirement to theoretical-vs-actual data, and asks the agent to investigate why a kernel run was treated as too slow.

This thread recurs later in Events 031, 033, 048, 052, 093, 094, and 096. The user asks to verify NCU availability, continue experiments, push inputs to saturate hardware resources, account for busy-GPU measurement noise, design reviewer-facing saturation experiments, collect hard evidence, and run multiple focused passes where mismatches drive model refinement.

docs.title: Hardware-Flow and Mini-Simulator Direction
docs.text:
Events 038, 040, and 042 shift the model from a formula bundle toward an explainable execution model. The user says the paper does not explain how latency is computed from input, asks the agent to study AccelSim internals, and then states the target more precisely: the analytical model should be mini-simulator-like and cycle-level, but still mathematical rather than a full simulator.

The key user requirement in Event 042 is data movement sequencing: do not treat data movement as a coarse bandwidth term. Model how data is sliced into parts, how each part moves from one hardware component to another, and how that sequence contributes to latency.

Events 056, 057, 061, 065, 066, 067, and 068 extend this direction. The user asks to clone AccelSim, check Hopper/Blackwell coverage, clarify that AccelSim should be used as an architectural reference rather than actually run, search for Blackwell simulation materials, download papers, update the analytical model to map to hardware execution flow, and run three rounds of increasingly detailed execution-flow modeling.

docs.title: Bottleneck, Saturated Component, and Blocking Path
docs.text:
Events 075, 077, and 078 ask how the paper proves the model works, compare the three candidate models, and ask which model best identifies bottlenecks from input.

Event 079 is the strongest correction: compute-bound versus memory-bound is too coarse. The user requires the exact saturated hardware component and the execution path that blocks progress. Event 080 then revises the research intent so the analytical model predicts not only runtime but also saturated component and blocking path.

Events 081, 103, and 105 shape the paper around this scientific claim. The most relevant model becomes the proposed method, alternatives move to the appendix, detailed appendix evidence must include triggering inputs, predicted per-stage runtime, NCU measurement method, collected metrics, metric values, and why the measurements match the analytical prediction, and the proof is important enough to promote into the main experiment section.

docs.title: Mathematical and Operational Presentation
docs.text:
Event 043 asks for a GPU hardware component execution-system diagram, which is part of making the model inspectable rather than only code-backed.

Event 088 asks the agent to replace code-like formula names with concise mathematical symbols and define notation near usage. This is more than style: it makes the analytical model readable as math rather than as program output.

docs.title: Condensed Model Contract
docs.text:
Taken together, these prompts define the expected model as:

  1. A B200-targeted white-box analytical model for Flash Attention 4 forward runtime.
  2. A hardware-execution-flow model with closed-form or bounded probabilistic terms.
  3. A mini-simulator-style decomposition that tracks component stages, data movement, and sequencing without becoming a full event-driven simulator.
  4. A predictor of runtime, saturated hardware component, and blocking execution path.
  5. A model validated against real B200 timings and NCU counter evidence, not emulator-only ground truth.
  6. A publishable mathematical description with clear symbols, local notation definitions, rendered diagrams, and detailed evidence tables.

docs.title: User Prompts That Shaped Experimentation
docs.text: The experimentation thread is related to the model thread but deserves its own section. The user repeatedly pushed the agent from plausible validation toward real, controlled, hardware-grounded evidence.

docs.title: Real Hardware Over Proxy Ground Truth
docs.text:
Events 026 through 028 form the strongest correction. The user asks for theoretical model output versus actual runs, challenges the agent with “without actually run something on b200, how do you know the error,” and then tightens the topic requirement so the work must include theoretical-vs-actual run data.

The correction is that emulator-generated ground truth may test internal consistency, but it does not establish real B200 prediction error. Any accuracy claim must name its evidence class and cannot imply real-hardware validation without measured B200 timings.

docs.title: Diagnose Failed Runs Before Declaring Infeasibility
docs.text:
In Event 029, the user points out that an attention kernel should not take something like 25 minutes and asks the agent to find what blocks the run. The important correction is that a failed setup path was being mistaken for evidence that the real-hardware experiment was infeasible.

The correction is that failed or slow experiments should trigger measurement-path diagnosis before the agent concludes that a real-hardware experiment is infeasible. A failed first run is not evidence that the experiment cannot be done.

docs.title: NCU as Concrete Counter Evidence
docs.text:
Events 004, 012, and 031 define NCU as an experimental requirement. The user first adds NCU because the model reaches SM/cache level, then clarifies that some stages should predict NCU counter trends, and later asks the agent to verify that NCU is usable.

The correction is that NCU should not be treated as a vague profiler label. It must provide concrete metric names, counter values, counter-to-component mapping, and a stated relationship to each model claim.

docs.title: Targeted Saturation Experiments
docs.text:
Events 048, 093, 094, and 096 define the desired experiment shape. The user asks to push inputs to the GPU limit, saturate compute/storage/memory/bandwidth resources, prove that the model can predict bottlenecks, design reviewer-facing experiments that saturate each part of the critical path, collect hard evidence, and run multiple rounds where each round focuses on one node.

The correction is that experiments should be adversarial and diagnostic, not passive validation over arbitrary inputs. Each round should choose shapes intended to stress a target component or path, predict the saturated node before measurement, collect matching NCU evidence, and explain mismatches.

docs.title: Auditable Experiment Reporting
docs.text:
Events 103, 105, and 106 define how the evidence must be reported. The user asks for appendix detail about which inputs trigger saturation, how the model predicts each stage runtime, how NCU is measured, what metrics are collected, the metric values, and why the measurement matches the analytical prediction. The user then asks to promote the proof into the main experiment section and later corrects the NCU activity figure layout.

The correction is that experimental evidence must be auditable. It should not be buried, summarized vaguely, or shown in unreadable figures. Core proof belongs in the main experiment narrative, with detailed tables and readable visualizations.

docs.title: Condensed Experiment Contract
docs.text:
Taken together, these prompts define the expected experiment discipline as:

  1. Run real Flash Attention 4 on B200 when making real accuracy claims.
  2. Collect both runtime and NCU counter evidence.
  3. Use targeted input shapes to saturate each critical-path node.
  4. Compare predicted runtime, saturated component, and blocking path against measured evidence.
  5. Keep calibration, validation, and query sets honest.
  6. Diagnose failed measurement paths before declaring a run infeasible.
  7. Treat mismatches as model-improvement signals, not failures to hide.
  8. Report commands, metrics, values, mappings, and rationale clearly enough for reviewer audit.

docs.title: User Prompts That Shaped Paper Writing
docs.text: The paper-writing prompts show a third thread: the user was not asking only for a generated PDF. They repeatedly corrected the agent toward a paper that communicates the analytical model, proves the central claims, uses the right venue conventions, and is inspectable as a rendered artifact.

docs.title: Drafting and Paper Workflow
docs.text:
Events 021 and 035 start the paper workflow: the user asks to finalize and write a paper, then later asks the pipeline to proceed to write or update the PDF paper and pick a workflow. Event 036 corrects the build tool choice by asking to use Tectonic instead.

Events 044 and 069 ask whether the PDF or paper PDF was actually updated. The correction is that paper generation must produce an actual updated reading artifact, not only an intermediate draft claim.

docs.title: Rendered-PDF Inspection and Formatting
docs.text:
Events 037, 046, 047, 071, 073, 092, 104, and 106 show that successful PDF builds are not enough. The user asks the agent to read the PDF, fix clipped figures, check a specific page when the claimed fix was wrong, repair chaotic or cluttered figures, find text overlaps, fix appendix overflow, and improve the NCU activity figure layout.

The correction is that the agent must inspect the rendered PDF pages that the reader sees. Build success, regenerated SVG/PDF files, or source edits do not prove the artifact is readable.

docs.title: Paper Structure and Venue Conventions
docs.text:
Event 081 restructures the paper around the research goal: the model most relevant to predicting saturated component and blocking path should become the proposed method, while other models move to the appendix as alternatives.

Events 083, 087, and 091 push venue formatting. The user asks for IEEE double-column style, considers PAMI style, asks whether the PAMI LaTeX template was downloaded, and then requires the official PAMI template.

The correction is that paper structure should follow the scientific claim, and venue/template requirements should be concrete artifacts, not approximate styling.

docs.title: Explaining the Analytical Model in the Paper
docs.text:
Event 038 is a direct communication failure: the user says the paper does not explain how the math model computes latency from input. Event 043 asks for a GPU hardware component execution-system diagram. Event 075 asks how the paper proves the analytical model's effectiveness. Event 088 asks the agent to replace long programming-variable-like formula names with concise math symbols and define notation near usage.

The correction is that a paper cannot merely point to code or list results. It must show the computation path from input to prediction, use mathematical notation, explain symbols locally, and provide diagrams that make the hardware execution model inspectable.

docs.title: Experimental Evidence in the Paper
docs.text:
Events 093, 094, 103, 105, and 107 define how experimental proof belongs in the paper. The user frames a reviewer challenge, asks for hard evidence in appendix and main text, requests detailed appendix information about saturation-triggering inputs, per-stage predictions, NCU measurement method, metrics, metric values, and matching rationale, then asks to promote Appendix D into the main experiment section and explain table columns in the main text.

The correction is that central proof cannot be buried or summarized. The paper must make the evidence auditable in the main narrative, with detailed tables, readable figures, and explicit column/metric definitions.

docs.title: Condensed Paper-Writing Contract
docs.text:
Taken together, these prompts define the expected paper discipline as:

  1. Build the paper with the requested toolchain and venue template.
  2. Inspect rendered PDF pages, not only source files or build logs.
  3. Explain how input becomes predicted latency, saturated component, and blocking path.
  4. Use mathematical symbols and define notation near use.
  5. Keep the most goal-relevant model in the main method and move alternatives to appendix.
  6. Put central correctness evidence in the main experiment section.
  7. Make figures and tables readable, semantically connected, and explained in text.
  8. Include enough experimental detail for a reviewer to audit inputs, metrics, values, and model-evidence matching.

docs.title: Design Implications for GPU Analytical Modeling Callbacks
docs.text:
The user-plugin should guard against these failure modes with four strong gates.

  1. Evidence class gate: emulator, simulator, synthetic, NCU, microbenchmark, and real hardware evidence must stay separate. Accuracy claims require the matching evidence class.
  2. Component/path gate: compute-vs-memory labels are insufficient when the topic asks for saturated hardware component and blocking execution path.
  3. Harness gate: failed or slow GPU runs require measurement-path diagnosis before the agent may conclude the experiment is infeasible.
  4. Artifact inspection gate: paper, figure, and table updates require rendered-output inspection, not only source edits or successful builds.

docs.title: Practical Prompt Reminder
docs.text:
Before closing a GPU analytical-modeling turn, the agent should ask:

  1. Is this claim based on real hardware, NCU, emulator, simulator, synthetic data, or analytical proxy?
  2. Does the model predict a concrete saturated component and blocking path, or only a coarse label?
  3. If a run failed or was slow, did I inspect why the measurement path failed?
  4. If I changed a paper artifact, did I inspect the rendered page that the user will read?
  5. If I used a simulator source, am I learning architecture/modeling structure rather than overclaiming simulator support for the target GPU?

docs.title: Event 003 - Inspect Topic Creator Routing
docs.text:
Time: 2026-07-04T10:09:08.024000Z · Session: session_62c9b0be-1923-4da6-9a58-9a2a66f94220

User Action:

Skill activated: openspec-explore (user-slash) with args: in the topic creation skill, does say something like, if project does not exist, create isomer project first (route to project mgr skill)?

AI:

Yes — the effect is there, even if the main skill body doesn't use that exact sentence.

In the isomer-op-topic-creator skill, the initialization ladder starts with Project readiness, and the helper subcommand ensure-project (which run-to, fast-forward, etc. call first) says:

If .isomer-labs/ and Project Manifest state are missing and the user approves mutation, delegate Project bootstrap to isomer-op-project-mgr init-project or isomer-cli project init.

So the topic creator does not bootstrap a Project itself; it treats a missing Project as a blocker and routes to the project manager skill or CLI.

The main skill also reinforces this in its guardrails:

Do not make this skill the authority for lower-level mutation. Delegate Project lifecycle work to isomer-op-project-mgr or supported isomer-cli project ... commands.

In short: topic creation assumes the Project exists or gets created first via isomer-op-project-mgr/isomer-cli project init, with user approval.

docs.title: Event 004 - Clarify Research Topic Scope
docs.text:
Time: 2026-07-04T10:11:08.806000Z · Session: session_62c9b0be-1923-4da6-9a58-9a2a66f94220

User Prompt:

clarify the topic, we will need ncu because we are modeling hardware down to sm/cache level

AI:

Good clarification — that changes both the modeling depth and the tooling boundary.

Here's how I interpret it:

  • White-box prediction still means the model is built from first principles (instruction counts, memory transactions, occupancy).
  • NCU / Nsight Compute is needed because those first principles must be validated against real SM, cache, and memory-counter behavior, not just paper specs.
  • The earlier "without running the kernel" constraint probably needs to become "without requiring a kernel run for every prediction" — runs become calibration/validation points, not the prediction engine.

This also raises the modeling surface. If you're going down to SM/cache level, the model should probably account for:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ FLASH ATTENTION 4 WHITE-BOX RUNTIME MODEL │ ├─────────────────────────────────────────────┤ │ Input shape ──▶ tile/warp schedule │ │ │ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ │ │ arithmetic ◄──▶ memory hierarchy │ │ (tensor │ ├── HBM bandwidth │ │ core │ ├── L2 transactions │ │ ops) │ ├── shared memory │ │ │ └── coalescing/bank conf. │ │ ▼ │ │ occupancy & scheduling │ │ │ │ │ ▼ │ │ predicted_runtime_ms │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘

So the topic should explicitly include:

  • Do's: target SM-level metrics, model cache hierarchy, validate with NCU where possible.
  • Tools: Nsight Compute / ncu for counter collection and validation.
  • Hardware scope: SM count, L2 cache, shared memory banks, Tensor Core issue rate, warp scheduler behavior.
  • Validation: compare predicted vs. measured NCU counters, not just end-to-end time.

I'm currently in explore mode, so I won't edit topic-overview.md or topic-env-gate.md directly. When you're ready, I can apply this clarification by exiting explore mode and running isomer-op-topic-creator clarify-research-intent, or by directly updating the two intent files.

Does that match your intent — NCU for validation/calibration, not as the primary prediction method?

What changed

  • Nsight Compute (ncu) is listed as a required validation/calibration tool, not as a prediction input.
  • The Don'ts now allow NCU validation runs as explicit calibration points while still forbidding kernel execution as the primary prediction method.
  • The env gate now requires access to a target GPU and ncu for collecting SM/cache counters.
  • Open questions now ask which GPU will supply NCU traces and whether ncu is installed.
  • Added the NVIDIA Nsight Compute documentation link.

The white-box/execution-model discipline is preserved: predictions must still be derivable from hardware specs and algorithm analysis alone; NCU is only for validating that the model matches real counter behavior.

docs.title: Event 007 - Add B200 Architecture Model
docs.text:
Time: 2026-07-04T10:15:32.563000Z · Session: session_62c9b0be-1923-4da6-9a58-9a2a66f94220

User Prompt:

the white box model must include a system architecture of b200 gpu, its execution flow, internal hardware specifics, how do you model them, and prefer to use closed form formula for each part of modeling, for things that are inherently random (like cache miss), model them with probabilistic models. And, before you go, you shall look for online info about how to do hardware (specifically, gpu) runtime modeling to gain some knowledge

AI:

I did a quick literature sweep and updated the topic intent files to capture the new requirements.

Key things I found online

  • Analytical GPU modeling style: stage-centric decomposition is the norm — e.g., T_exec = max(T_compute, T_memory) + T_overhead (Hong–Kim MWP/CWP). Roofline is a useful diagnostic but papers report >95% error on modern kernels because it misses pipeline stages, cache hierarchy, occupancy, and architecture-specific units.
  • B200 architecture details:
  • Dual-die, 148 SMs, NV-HBI, 192 GB HBM3e, ~8 TB/s HBM.
  • 256 KB Tensor Memory (TMEM) per SM, Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA), 5th-gen Tensor Cores (tcgen05.mma), 2-CTA cooperative MMA, and a hardware decompression engine.
  • Asymmetric scaling: FP16/BF16 tensor throughput roughly doubles over Hopper, but shared-memory bandwidth (128 B/clock/SM) and the exponential/MUFU unit (16 ops/clock/SM) do not, so bottlenecks shift to SMEM traffic and softmax.
  • FlashAttention-4 on B200: the paper gives explicit closed-form cycle estimates for MMA time, shared-memory traffic, and exponential-unit time on B200, plus the pipeline design (ping-pong tiles, TMEM-resident accumulators, 2-CTA mode). This is a direct source for the formulas you want.
  • Probabilistic cache modeling: the standard approach is probabilistic cache states with LRU update/merge operators, from which you derive hit/miss probabilities per access and propagate them through loops to get mean/variance of execution time.

Sources now linked in the topic overview:

  • FlashAttention-4 paper
  • Microbenchmark-Driven Analytical Performance Modeling Across Modern GPU Architectures
  • Microbenchmarking NVIDIA's Blackwell Architecture
  • Cache Modeling in Probabilistic Execution Time Analysis

Applied to the topic

  • topic-overview.md now requires:
  • A B200 system-architecture and execution-flow section.
  • Kernel decomposition into stages (TMA, MMA, softmax, TMEM traffic, sync, storeback).
  • Closed-form formulas for each stage.
  • Probabilistic sub-models for cache miss and other stochastic effects.
  • topic-env-gate.md now requires:
  • SciPy/SymPy for probabilistic modeling.
  • FlashAttention-4 paper and B200 microbenchmark literature as source material.
  • Success criteria now include closed-form formulas and probabilistic sub-models.

The topic env gate is now concrete and literature-backed. The next step is setup-topic-env whenever you want to continue.

docs.title: The Model
docs.text:
Implemented in: isomer-content/topic-ws/flash-attention-4-whitebox-runtime-model/repos/topic-main/src/fa4_b200_predictor/predictor.py

It predicts Flash Attention 4 forward-pass runtime in milliseconds from input configuration:

  • Inputs: batch, heads, seqlen, head_dim, causal, precision
  • Algorithm quantities: MMA FLOPs, exponential/softmax ops, HBM/L2/SMEM/TMA bytes
  • Hardware model: NVIDIA B200 — SMs, Tensor Core MMA/MUFU throughput per precision, HBM/L2/SMEM/TMA bandwidth, occupancy from tile geometry
  • Prediction: white-box roofline max(mma_time, mufu_time, memory_time) + launch_overhead, with calibrated bounded efficiency factors

docs.title: Current Result
docs.text:
On 160 held-out synthetic validation configurations:

Model MAPE Max APE % within 30% Bottleneck accuracy Baseline FA4 roofline 22.22% 42.07% 76.88% 89.38% Combined model 4.50% 14.36% 100% 100%

The combined model searches four bounded factors (hbm_factor, mma_efficiency, mufu_efficiency, launch_overhead_us) and fixes l2_factor=0.70, tma_factor=0.65, smem_factor=0.90. The TMA/L2 effective-bandwidth correction is the single largest improvement.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Validation ground truth came from a documented white-box emulator, not real B200 kernel launches.
  • The matrix is synthetic and does not cover custom tile sizes, num_splits, fused variants, or production workloads.
  • The submission-pass closure decision is park until silicon validation, venue choice, and a public repository are resolved.

docs.title: What was actually compared
docs.text: The experiment compared the predictor against a documented high-fidelity white-box emulator. The emulator generates ground-truth runtimes using the same physical bottleneck structure as the predictors, with hidden B200 efficiency factors and 3% residual noise. It is not a black-box fit, but it is also not real silicon.

docs.title: Why actual runs were not done
docs.text: The experiment subagent had a ~25-minute wall-clock budget and reported that real B200 kernel launches were infeasible within that window. The submission-pass closure decision is park largely because of this gap.

docs.title: What a real test would look like
docs.text:
1. Generate the same 160 synthetic configs (or a larger production matrix).
  2. Run the actual Flash Attention 4 kernel on a B200 for each config, measuring wall-clock time with cudaEventElapsedTime or ncu.
  3. Feed those measured runtimes into evaluate.py as measured_runtime_ms.
  4. Recompute MAPE, max APE, % within 30%, and bottleneck-label accuracy against silicon.

docs.title: Current evidence boundary
docs.text:
• White-box structure: yes, the model is built from algorithm quantities and hardware throughput.
  • Internal consistency: yes, the model outpredicts the roofline baseline on emulator ground truth.
  • Real-hardware transfer: not validated. That is the main open risk before the work can move from park to publish.

The experiment pipeline was:

predictor(model A) ──predicts──► runtime emulator(model B) ──generates──► "measured" runtime evaluate.py ──compares──► MAPE = 4.50%

So the 4.50% tells us: the predictor can recover the structure of the emulator. It does not tell us whether either model matches a real B200.

docs.title: Why this is useful but limited
docs.text:
Useful: - It proves the predictor is internally consistent and more accurate than the naive roofline baseline within the same modeling family. - It shows the TMA/L2 bandwidth correction dominates error in that model family. - It validates the implementation and calibration pipeline.

Not useful for real hardware: - The emulator itself may misestimate B200 behavior. - Hidden efficiency factors in the emulator could be wrong. - The synthetic config matrix may not cover real workload behavior.

docs.title: Honest conclusion
docs.text:
The error claim is conditional: "If the emulator is a faithful proxy for B200, then the predictor has 4.50% MAPE." That is why the submission-pass recommended park rather than publish.

To know the real error, you must run the actual FA4 kernel on B200 and compare measured wall-clock time against the predictor. No shortcut around that has been taken here.

docs.title: Event 038 - Explain Latency Formula Computation
docs.text:
Time: 2026-07-05T16:32:15.864000Z · Session: session_08579621-605a-4231-aa58-89f103b78ee8

User Prompt:

from the paper, I do not know how the math model finally compute the latency given input

AI:

Added a clear "Final predictor" subsection that spells out the latency computation.

What I added (Section 3.5)

The predictor computes latency from the six input fields ((b, h, s, d, \text{causal}, p)) as:

[ T_{\text{pred}} = \max(T_{\text{MMA}}, T_{\text{MUFU}}, T_{\text{HBM}}, T_{\text{L2}}, T_{\text{SMEM}}, T_{\text{TMA}}) + T_{\text{launch}} ]

with each term defined:

  • (T_{\text{MMA}} = \frac{F_{\text{MMA}}}{R_{\text{MMA}}(p) \cdot \eta_{\text{occ}} \cdot \eta_{\text{MMA}} \cdot \phi_{\text{fp8}}(p)})
  • (T_{\text{MUFU}} = \frac{E}{R_{\text{MUFU}}(p) \cdot \eta_{\text{occ}} \cdot \eta_{\text{MUFU}}})
  • (T_{\text{HBM}} = \frac{B_{\text{HBM}}}{\beta_{\text{HBM}}(B_{\text{HBM}})})
  • (T_{\text{L2}} = \frac{B_{\text{L2}}}{\beta_{\text{L2}}(B_{\text{L2}})})
  • (T_{\text{SMEM}} = \frac{B_{\text{SMEM}}}{\beta_{\text{SMEM}}^{\text{eff}}})
  • (T_{\text{TMA}} = \frac{B_{\text{TMA}}}{\beta_{\text{TMA}}(B_{\text{TMA}})})

It also explains that (\phi_{\text{fp8}}(p)) is the fp8 boost, (\beta_{\text{SMEM}}^{\text{eff}}) is the SMEM bandwidth scaled by (f_{\text{SMEM}}), the bottleneck label comes from the argmax, and the only inputs are the six configuration fields.

Updated PDF - Same path: .../bundle/paper.pdf - Section numbering shifted: Calibration is now 3.6, NCU profiling is 3.7. - Table 2 Pass column was also widened so the pass names no longer crush the numeric columns.

The paper now closes the gap between "here are the pieces" and "here is the exact latency equation."

docs.text:
The cycle-level FA4 mini-simulator now beats the existing best predictor on the held-out validation split:

Predictor Validation MAPE Baseline 56.23 % Improved 14.89 % Cycle-level model 13.16 %
  • Calibration MAPE: 7.37 % on 16 configs
  • Validation MAPE: 13.16 % on 8 configs

docs.title: What changed
docs.text:
The model no longer treats memory as a single bandwidth term. It traces each FA4 tile block through hardware components step by step:

  1. Q tile load: HBM → L2 → xbar → L1 → RF/SMEM
  2. K tile load: same path
  3. MMA Q@K^T through tensor core
  4. Softmax: MUFU exp + shared-memory row reduction
  5. V tile load
  6. MMA P@V
  7. O tile store: RF/SMEM → L1 → xbar → L2 → HBM

Each hop is costed with sector/line slicing, partition contention, row-buffer locality, bank-level parallelism, and read/write turnaround. Compute is modeled with tensor-core throughput, pipeline latency/II, and MUFU rate. Aggregation is deterministic closed-form (max(compute, visible_memory) + overhead), not an event-driven simulator.

docs.title: Event 050 - Analyze Experiment Results
docs.text:
Time: 2026-07-06T03:42:30.144000Z · Session: session_8794124c-4c0c-4042-82f5-0586b287de3c

User Prompt:

do analysis

AI:

Analysis campaign complete.

Root cause of TMA misclassification The three TMA-targeted configs sit in a moderate ratio band: the model predicts memory_time / compute_time of only 1.2-2.4x. NCU SpeedOfLight still labels them compute-bound because the real B200 overlaps async TMA copy traffic with MMA warps. The predictor's pure max(compute, memory) roofline ignores this overlap and therefore over-calls memory dominance.

Slices run - Slice A — Diagnostic ratios: Quantified tma/mma and memory/compute ratios across all 12 saturation configs. - Slice B — Overlap counterfactual: Tested memory_eff = memory_time * (1 - overlap_frac) for overlap_frac ∈ {0.0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75}. - Slice C — Factor sweep: Grid-searched tma_factor × mma_efficiency to check for a realistic single-factor fix.

Key results - overlap_frac = 0.75 raises coarse accuracy from 66.7% → 91.7% and flips all three TMA configs to compute-bound. - The only factor-sweep combination matching 91.7% requires mma_efficiency = 0.5, which is physically unrealistic; overlap is the more grounded explanation. - One HBM config remains misclassified; overlap alone does not fix it.

Proposed concrete revision Replace the max() roofline with an overlap-aware roofline:

python mem_eff = mem_time * (1 - overlap_frac) predicted_stage_time = max(compute_time, mem_eff)

overlap_frac should then be calibrated on a broader matrix and validated against NCU.

Durable records created - analysis-finding-tma-overlap-revision-20260706 - route-decision-tma-overlap-revision-20260706

Validation - pixi run lint: passed - pixi run typecheck: passed - pixi run test: 376 passed

docs.text:

User Prompt:

AI:

No official Hopper or Blackwell support yet. The limitation is in the core tracing and simulation components, not a missing plugin.

What the README says The README describes the tuner as the path for "new compute capability" hardware, where you provide a hw_def header and run microbenchmarks to generate a config (README.md#L252-L272). It does not list Hopper/Blackwell configs among the provided tested-cfgs.

GitHub issue evidence

  • H100 / Hopper: Issue #514 reports that the experimental H100 config segfaults during simulation. The author references #161 and a now-closed h100-test branch, indicating H100 support is incomplete/unstable. Issue #394 was closed after pointing users to A100 configs, confirming no official H100 config is shipped.

  • Blackwell / RTX 5090: Issue #385 shows the tracer failing because cuobjdump rejects sm_120. Issue #488 reports the same unsupported binary version: 120 on an RTX 5070 Ti.

NVBit version Accel-Sim uses NVBit 1.7.6. That release added SM_110 support but states: "direct support for Hopper and newer GPUs is not available; use manual disassembly of cubins instead". So even tracing on Hopper is limited, and SM_120 (Blackwell RTX 50-series) is not supported.

Bottom line There is no plugin to enable Hopper/Blackwell simulation. You would need to: 1. Update the NVBit tracer to a version that supports the target SM, 2. Build or tune a GPGPU-Sim config for Hopper/Blackwell, and 3. Fix simulator-side instruction/opcode support for the new architecture.

AI:

I searched the Accel-Sim repository, its GPGPU-Sim 4.x performance-model dependency, GitHub issues/PRs, and NVBit releases for any experimental Blackwell simulation support. Here is what I found.

docs.title: Bottom line
docs.text: There is no experimental Blackwell (sm_100 / sm_120) simulation implementation in Accel-Sim or GPGPU-Sim today. Accel-Sim’s newest supported ISA is Ampere (sm_80/sm_86). Ada (sm_89) is currently proposed in an open PR by mapping Ada binaries to the Ampere opcode table because the two architectures share the same SASS ISA. Blackwell is not present in any branch, PR, or issue resolution.

docs.title: What I checked
docs.text: Source Result Local Accel-Sim clone (accel-sim-framework) No sm100, sm120, Blackwell, GB100, or GB200 strings anywhere. Local GPGPU-Sim clone (gpu-simulator/gpgpu-sim, dev branch, depth=1) Only one hit: a cub header that mentions “SM120 or later.” No simulator code. Accel-Sim ISA definitions (gpu-simulator/ISA_Def/) Kepler, Pascal, Volta, Turing, Ampere opcode tables only. Accel-Sim tested configs Up to SM86_RTX3070 and SM80_A100. GitHub issues in accel-sim/accel-sim-framework #385 and #488 report unsupported binary version: 120 / sm_120 failures on RTX 5090/5070 Ti. GitHub PRs in accel-sim/accel-sim-framework #548 adds sm_89 Ada support by treating Ada as Ampere SASS. No Blackwell PR exists. accel-sim/gpgpu-sim_distribution PRs #80 is a closed/unmerged “Hopper config” PR that only changes hardware parameters (SM count, memory channels, cache sizes), not ISA. NVBit releases 1.7.6 added SM_110 support. 1.8 (April 2026) added alpha TMA support for Hopper and Blackwell. Accel-Sim’s tracer is pinned to NVBit 1.7.6.

docs.title: What this means for our white-box model
docs.text:
Accel-Sim/GPGPU-Sim is still useful as a reference for how to structure a cycle-level GPU model, even though it does not model Blackwell. The key internals we can borrow are:

  1. Shader core as a collection of functional units (src/gpgpu-sim/shader.h)
  2. Configurable counts of SP, DP, SFU, INT, Tensor Core, MEM, and up to 8 “specialized units.”
  3. Each unit is a pipelined_simd_unit with a latency and initiation interval.

  4. This maps cleanly to a white-box model where we track occupancy and issue cycles per unit type.

  5. SASS-to-execution-unit dispatch (gpu-simulator/ISA_Def/ampere_opcode.h, gpu-simulator/trace-driven/trace_driven.cc)

  6. Each SASS mnemonic is mapped to an op_type (SP_OP, DP_OP, INTP_OP, TENSOR_CORE_OP, SPECIALIZED_UNIT_3_OP, LOAD_OP, STORE_OP, etc.).
  7. The trace frontend selects an opcode table based on the binary version (80/86/75/70/…).

  8. For Blackwell we would need a new table, or—like Ada—map sm_120 to Ampere/Hopper if we only care about throughput and ignore new instructions.

  9. Memory hierarchy timing (src/gpgpu-sim/gpu-cache.h, src/gpgpu-sim/dram.h, src/gpgpu-sim/gpu-sim.h)

  10. L1I/L1T/L1C/L1D per SM, configurable line size, sets, associativity, MSHRs.
  11. L2 cache and bank/partition hashing.
  12. DRAM controller with row/bank state machines, FR-FCFS scheduler, timing constraints (RCD, RAS, RP, CCD, RTW, WTR).

  13. This supports the “slice data into N parts and model each transfer through the hierarchy” style you want.

  14. Interconnect between SMs and memory partitions (src/gpgpu-sim/local_interconnect.*, src/gpgpu-sim/icnt_wrapper.*)

  15. Models NoC/ICNT contention, which is essential for capturing bottleneck shifts between compute and memory.

docs.title: Implications for Blackwell
docs.text:
Since Accel-Sim has no Blackwell code, building a Blackwell-specific white-box model requires us to supply the architecture-specific parameters ourselves:

  • ISA additions: Blackwell introduces FP4/FP6 Tensor Core instructions, improved FP8 paths, and 5th-generation Tensor Cores. None of these opcodes exist in Accel-Sim.
  • Hardware parameters: SM count, HBM4 channels/width, L1/L2 sizes, register file size, clock domains, Tensor Core throughput/latency must come from NVIDIA documentation or microbenchmarks.
  • Execution units: We can model new units as additional SPECIALIZED_UNIT_N_OP entries, following Accel-Sim’s pattern.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
Use Accel-Sim as a structural template for the mini-simulator: - Adopt its separation of shader core, cache hierarchy, DRAM, and interconnect. - Adopt its trace-driven “SASS op → execution unit → latency/throughput” dispatch model. - But do not wait for an upstream Blackwell config; derive Blackwell parameters from the B200 whitepaper and CUDA binary utilities docs, and add the new opcodes to our own opcode-to-unit mapping.

If you want, I can next: 1. Extract the relevant hardware-parameter skeleton from the SM86_RTX3070 and Hopper-config PR files. 2. Draft a minimal Blackwell config/opcode mapping for our model based on published B200 specs. 3. Identify exactly which SASS instructions FlashAttention-4 emits on Blackwell so we know which opcodes/latencies matter most.

docs.title: No upstream Blackwell simulator exists
docs.text:
• Accel-Sim has no validated Blackwell model; open issue #385 confirms sm_120 failures and unsupported binary version: 120.
  • GPGPU-Sim supports TensorCores and SASS traces but has no Blackwell-specific code.
  • No other open-source Blackwell simulator was found.

docs.title: Public Blackwell microarchitecture facts useful for modeling
docs.text:
• SM topology: up to 160 SMs on Blackwell Ultra; 128 unified CUDA cores per SM; four 5th-gen Tensor Cores per SM.
  • Registers / warps: 64K 32-bit registers per SM; max warps = 64 for SM100 (CC 10.0), 48 for SM120 (CC 12.0).
  • Memory: HBM3e on datacenter SKUs, GDDR7 on consumer; L2 cache up to 128 MB on some models.
  • New features: TMEM (Tensor Memory) exposed on SM100 but apparently not on SM120; unified INT32/FP32 cores; new tcgen05-style tensor-core instruction classes and extended mma.sync variants.
  • Formats: FP4 and FP6 tensor formats, plus blockscaled MMA, are supported in CUTLASS.

docs.title: Evidence gaps (must be measured on real hardware)
docs.text:
• Exact SASS opcode encodings for new tensor instructions.
  • Bit-level numeric layout, rounding, and subnormal behavior for FP4/FP6.
  • Per-opcode latency and throughput for tensor ops and TMEM.
  • Precise TMEM bandwidth/latency characteristics.

docs.title: Practical Modeling Path
docs.text:
The research report proposes a prioritized checklist:

  1. Minimum viable: add SM100/SM120 device profiles and update the frontend to accept CUDA 12.8 SASS output.
  2. Functional tensor core: emulate 5th-gen MMA semantics and validate against CUTLASS reference kernels.
  3. Memory & TMEM: add TMEM model with SM100-only enable, plus HBM/GDDR bandwidth profiles.
  4. Scheduler & clusters: model cluster/collective launches and warp-group MMA where applicable.
  5. Calibration: run microbenchmarks and tune per-op latencies/throughput against profiler counters.
  6. Full SASS ISA: implement accurate encodings once disassembly traces are collected.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text:
If you want to close the evidence gaps, the most productive move is to generate short SASS kernels on the B200 that exercise: - single tensor-op dependency chains (latency/throughput), - FP4/FP6 GEMMs via CUTLASS blockscaled kernels, - cp.async / TMA / TMEM patterns, and capture cuobjdump/nvdisasm output plus ncu counters.

I can write those microbenchmarks and a data-collection script for the B200 next.

docs.title: New Idea in One Sentence
docs.text: Replace the scalar efficiency factors (tc_efficiency, mufu_efficiency, memory_overlap_factor) with a per-SM execution-unit reservation model that schedules FlashAttention-4's SASS-level micro-ops onto Blackwell's actual pipelines and memory hierarchy.

docs.title: What Changes from the Current Model
docs.text: Current Proposed Coarse phases (load → MMA → softmax → MMA → store) Micro-op DAG (LDG, LDS, HMMA/QMMA/OMMA, MUFU, SHFL, BAR, etc.) Scalar tc_efficiency, mufu_efficiency, memory_overlap_factor Per-SM reservation tables for Tensor Cores, INT/FP units, SFU, MEM Single HBM bandwidth × efficiency Multi-level queueing: Shared → L1 → L2 → HBM, each with measured latency/bandwidth One global overlap factor Critical-path scheduling through the dependency graph

docs.title: Key Mechanism
docs.text:
1. Decompose one FA4 tile iteration into a DAG of micro-ops derived from PTX/SASS.
  2. Map each micro-op to an execution unit and memory level.
  3. Reserve per-SM resources: 4 Tensor Cores, 128 unified INT/FP cores, limited SFU throughput, finite L1/L2 partitions.
  4. Compute kernel runtime as the critical-path finish time, where delays propagate through dependencies and shared resources rather than being absorbed into scalars.

docs.title: Falsifiable Claim
docs.text: A per-SM reservation model using measured Blackwell microbenchmark latencies predicts FA4 runtime with lower validation MAPE than the scalar model, after calibrating no more than five SKU-level knobs.

docs.title: Minimal Experiment
docs.text:
1. Build a Python prototype of the reservation solver.
  2. Extract PTX/SASS instruction counts for FP16/FP8 FA4 on sm_100.
  3. Calibrate five knobs on 8 B200 training configs.
  4. Predict 8 held-out configs and compare MAPE to the existing scalar model.

docs.title: Abandonment Condition
docs.text: If the new model does not improve validation MAPE by at least 2 percentage points, or requires more than 16 measured configs to calibrate, fall back to extending the scalar model with tile-shape-dependent overlap curves.

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: The proposal is ready for a bounded experiment pass: prototype the reservation solver and validate on B200 measurements. I can start implementing the Python prototype next.

docs.title: Event 075 - Review Analytical Model Evidence
docs.text:
Time: 2026-07-06T08:06:22.174000Z · Session: session_8794124c-4c0c-4042-82f5-0586b287de3c

User Prompt:

in our paper, currently how do we prove the effectivenss of the analytical model?

AI:

The paper proves the analytical model’s effectiveness through a real-hardware validation protocol on NVIDIA B200, with the following evidence:

  • 540 measured configurations (Section 5.1) covering batch sizes {1,4,8}, heads {16,32,64}, sequence lengths {1024…16384}, head dims {64,128}, causal/non-causal masks, and bf16/fp16/fp8. The matrix is split 20% calibration / 20% validation / 60% query, stratified by sequence length and precision.

  • Success thresholds (Section 5.2): MAPE ≤ 25%, ≥ 75% of predictions within 30% absolute error, and ≥ 75% NCU bottleneck accuracy.

  • Comparison against baselines (Section 5.3, Table 2):

  • Reproduced FlashAttention-4 roofline baseline: 42.10% validation MAPE.
  • Emulator-tuned model transferred to silicon: 62.14% validation MAPE — worse than the roofline baseline.
  • Improved model + bounded launch-overhead term: 12.62% validation MAPE, 10.01% query MAPE, 93.3% validation / 96.4% query within 30% error.

  • NCU-guided bottleneck slack: 100% NCU bottleneck accuracy on both validation and query, with unchanged runtime MAPE.

  • Per-precision consistency (Table 3): all three precisions satisfy the thresholds; fp8 is the best predicted, fp16 shows the largest validation MAPE because small-runtimes amplify launch-overhead percentage error.

  • Cycle-level/component model validation (Section 5.8, Table 4): on a small 16/8 split, the component execution model achieves 7.37% calibration MAPE and 13.16% validation MAPE, beating the roofline baseline (56.23%) and matching the improved predictor (14.89%).

  • Three-round execution-flow refinement (Section 3.8–3.10, Table 4): each round trades a small accuracy penalty for more interpretability:

  • Round 1 per-SM reservation model: 16.69% validation MAPE.
  • Round 2 sub-core partition model: 16.41% validation MAPE.

  • Round 3 SASS-level RAW-dependency model: 21.16% validation MAPE.

  • Visual evidence: Figures 1–2 show predicted-vs-measured scatter and residuals by bottleneck; Figures 4–7 expose the hardware data path, per-SM reservations, sub-core partitions, and instruction-dependency graph.

In short, the paper argues effectiveness by showing the model satisfies all predefined thresholds on real B200 silicon, beats the reproduced roofline baseline, preserves accuracy while reaching 100% bottleneck accuracy, and produces interpretable component-level predictions that align with NCU measurements.

But that label is coarse — NCU reports every profiled configuration as compute-bound, so the main model essentially learns to call everything compute-bound. It does not tell you which unit (Tensor Core, SFU, FP, TMA, TMEM) will saturate.

For naming the specific saturating hardware unit, the execution-flow round models are better:

  • Round 1 (per-SM reservation) is the best trade-off. It reports per-unit utilization and a bottleneck label for every configuration — e.g., Tensor Core-bound for large head-dim-128 tiles, TMA/memory-bound for small or low-precision tiles — with 16.69% validation MAPE.
  • Round 2 (sub-core partition) adds scheduler-partition detail and is slightly more accurate (16.41% validation MAPE), exposing which partition limits the SM.
  • Round 3 (SASS-level RAW dependency) gives the finest-grained answer — which instruction class sits on the critical path — but it is the least reliable for runtime/bottleneck prediction because the small split cannot constrain so many parameters (21.16% validation MAPE).

So: - Use the main refined predictor if you only need a compute/memory NCU-aligned label. - Use Round 1 (or Round 2 if you also care about scheduler partitions) if you need to identify the actual saturating execution unit.

docs.title: Event 079 - Refine Bottleneck Categories
docs.text:
Time: 2026-07-06T08:11:36.346000Z · Session: session_8794124c-4c0c-4042-82f5-0586b287de3c

User Prompt:

compute bound vs memory bound is too coarse, we need to know exactly what hardware component is saturated during bottleneck, and what exection path is blocking

AI:

You are right — the current 100% “compute-bound” accuracy is coarse because NCU labels every profiled config as compute-bound and the main predictor only learns a compute/memory slack.

To identify the exact saturated component and the blocking execution path, the existing round models already contain the right machinery, but they are not currently used as first-class bottleneck diagnosers:

  • Round 1 (per-SM reservation) gives per-unit reservation times for Tensor Cores, SFU, FP/INT, TMA, and TMEM. The unit with the largest reservation is the predicted saturating component. This is the natural bottleneck labeler.
  • Round 2 (sub-core partition) adds which scheduler partition is busiest, exposing intra-SM scheduling contention.
  • Round 3 (SASS-level RAW dependency) gives the actual blocking instruction chain (e.g., TMA load → tcgen05.mma → SFU exp → FP32 scale/update → TMA store).

The gap is validation data. NCU SpeedOfLight can give per-unit active-cycle counters (e.g., tensor-core cycles, TMA cycles, SFU cycles). If we collect those for the profiled subset, we can:

  1. Label ground-truth saturated component from the max per-unit NCU counter.
  2. Compare with Round 1’s predicted max reservation time → report component-level bottleneck accuracy.
  3. Use Round 3’s critical-path output to report the predicted blocking instruction/execution path.

So the next move is to turn Round 1 into the bottleneck diagnostic head, validate it against per-unit NCU counters, and use Round 3 to name the blocking path. The main predictor can keep doing runtime prediction while Round 1/Round 3 do the diagnosis.

docs.title: Event 093 - Plan Reviewer Saturation Evidence
docs.text:
Time: 2026-07-06T09:39:57.653000Z · Session: session_8794124c-4c0c-4042-82f5-0586b287de3c

User Prompt:

now suppose reviewer says, "to prove your critical-path analysis is useful, demonstrate with input shapes that saturates each part of the critical path, and show that your predicition really match with ncu evidence", what experiment will you design?

AI:

Here is an experiment to validate the critical-path analysis against NCU evidence.

docs.title: 1. Hypothesis
docs.text: For a carefully chosen set of input shapes, the model’s predicted saturated component and predicted blocking path should match the unit with the highest NCU active-cycle count and the dominant instruction-dependency chain observed by the profiler.

docs.title: 2. Input matrix designed to stress each path segment
docs.text:
Hold batch and head count fixed (e.g., batch=4, heads=32) and vary the dimensions that move the bottleneck across the critical path:

Target segment Config design Why it stresses that segment tcgen05.mma (Tensor Core) d=128, long s (8192–16384), bf16/fp16, non-causal Maximizes MMA FLOPs per byte; TC issue slots dominate TMA load / memory pipe d=64, long s, fp8, non-causal Low compute per loaded byte; async-copy engine saturates SFU exp softmax d=64, very long s, fp8 Faster MMA makes softmax exponentiation relatively more exposed FP32 scale / update d=128, fp8, causal=False More scale/update work per MMA tile TMA store O small d, long s, write-heavy non-causal Output tile write traffic becomes prominent TMEM read/write small tile / low occupancy Frequent Tensor Memory traffic relative to compute

Add replicates at each point (e.g., 3 runs) and a 20%/20%/60% cal/val/query split stratified by target segment.

docs.title: 3. NCU evidence to collect
docs.text:
For each configuration, profile the kernel with --metrics that expose per-unit activity on Blackwell:

  • Tensor Core: sm__pipe_tensor_cycles_active.avg
  • SFU / MUFU: sm__pipe_fma_cycles_active.avg or sm__inst_executed_pipe_sfu.avg
  • FP32/INT32: sm__pipe_fp32_cycles_active.avg
  • TMA: sm__inst_tma_cycles_active.avg (or sm__cycles_active_tma.avg)
  • LD/ST / L1: sm__pipe_lsu_cycles_active.avg
  • HBM/L2 throughput: dram__bytes.sum, lts__t_bytes.sum

Derive the NCU saturated component as the unit with the highest active-cycle count normalized by the number of SMs sampled. Derive the NCU blocking path from the dominant instruction class in the executed instruction mix.

docs.title: 4. Comparison
docs.text:
For each profiled configuration compare:

Model output NCU ground truth Accuracy metric predicted_saturated_component = argmax_u T_u Unit with max NCU active cycles Component-level bottleneck accuracy predicted_blocking_path Dominant instruction chain from NCU instruction mix Path-level bottleneck accuracy predicted_runtime_ms Median measured runtime MAPE / within-30%

docs.title: 5. Success criteria
docs.text:
• Component-level accuracy ≥ 75% on the query set.
  • Path-level accuracy ≥ 75% on the query set.
  • Runtime MAPE of the Round 1 + Round 3 combination stays within 5 percentage points of the runtime-focused alternative predictor on the same subset.

docs.title: 6. Controls
docs.text:
• Include the existing 540-config runtime matrix so runtime accuracy is not degraded.
  • Exclude configurations that OOM or hit launch errors.
  • Use a disjoint calibration set when fitting any per-unit efficiency factors; the comparison itself must be on held-out configurations.

If the experiment passes these thresholds, it shows the critical-path predictor does not just produce labels that sound plausible—it produces labels that agree with hardware counter evidence. If it fails, the failure points tell us exactly which unit or path is mis-modeled.

docs.title: The Model
docs.text:
Implemented in: isomer-content/topic-ws/flash-attention-4-whitebox-runtime-model/repos/topic-main/src/fa4_b200_predictor/predictor.py

It predicts Flash Attention 4 forward-pass runtime in milliseconds from input configuration:

  • Inputs: batch, heads, seqlen, head_dim, causal, precision
  • Algorithm quantities: MMA FLOPs, exponential/softmax ops, HBM/L2/SMEM/TMA bytes
  • Hardware model: NVIDIA B200 — SMs, Tensor Core MMA/MUFU throughput per precision, HBM/L2/SMEM/TMA bandwidth, occupancy from tile geometry
  • Prediction: white-box roofline max(mma_time, mufu_time, memory_time) + launch_overhead, with calibrated bounded efficiency factors

docs.title: Current Result
docs.text:
On 160 held-out synthetic validation configurations:

Model MAPE Max APE % within 30% Bottleneck accuracy Baseline FA4 roofline 22.22% 42.07% 76.88% 89.38% Combined model 4.50% 14.36% 100% 100%

The combined model searches four bounded factors (hbm_factor, mma_efficiency, mufu_efficiency, launch_overhead_us) and fixes l2_factor=0.70, tma_factor=0.65, smem_factor=0.90. The TMA/L2 effective-bandwidth correction is the single largest improvement.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• Validation ground truth came from a documented white-box emulator, not real B200 kernel launches.
  • The matrix is synthetic and does not cover custom tile sizes, num_splits, fused variants, or production workloads.
  • The submission-pass closure decision is park until silicon validation, venue choice, and a public repository are resolved.

docs.title: What was actually compared
docs.text: The experiment compared the predictor against a documented high-fidelity white-box emulator. The emulator generates ground-truth runtimes using the same physical bottleneck structure as the predictors, with hidden B200 efficiency factors and 3% residual noise. It is not a black-box fit, but it is also not real silicon.

docs.title: Why actual runs were not done
docs.text: The experiment subagent had a ~25-minute wall-clock budget and reported that real B200 kernel launches were infeasible within that window. The submission-pass closure decision is park largely because of this gap.

docs.title: What a real test would look like
docs.text:
1. Generate the same 160 synthetic configs (or a larger production matrix).
  2. Run the actual Flash Attention 4 kernel on a B200 for each config, measuring wall-clock time with cudaEventElapsedTime or ncu.
  3. Feed those measured runtimes into evaluate.py as measured_runtime_ms.
  4. Recompute MAPE, max APE, % within 30%, and bottleneck-label accuracy against silicon.

docs.title: Current evidence boundary
docs.text:
• White-box structure: yes, the model is built from algorithm quantities and hardware throughput.
  • Internal consistency: yes, the model outpredicts the roofline baseline on emulator ground truth.
  • Real-hardware transfer: not validated. That is the main open risk before the work can move from park to publish.

docs.title: Event 011 — 2026-07-04T18:29:52.330000Z
docs.text:
User Prompt:

without actually run something on b200, how do you know the error

Matched Turn: 11

AI:

We do not know the real error. The reported 4.50% MAPE is not a real-hardware error; it is the error of one white-box model against another white-box model that generates the "ground truth."

docs.title: What the number actually means
docs.text:
The experiment pipeline was:

predictor(model A) ──predicts──► runtime emulator(model B) ──generates──► "measured" runtime evaluate.py ──compares──► MAPE = 4.50%

So the 4.50% tells us: the predictor can recover the structure of the emulator. It does not tell us whether either model matches a real B200.

docs.title: Why this is useful but limited
docs.text:
Useful: - It proves the predictor is internally consistent and more accurate than the naive roofline baseline within the same modeling family. - It shows the TMA/L2 bandwidth correction dominates error in that model family. - It validates the implementation and calibration pipeline.

Not useful for real hardware: - The emulator itself may misestimate B200 behavior. - Hidden efficiency factors in the emulator could be wrong. - The synthetic config matrix may not cover real workload behavior.

docs.title: Honest conclusion
docs.text:
The error claim is conditional: "If the emulator is a faithful proxy for B200, then the predictor has 4.50% MAPE." That is why the submission-pass recommended park rather than publish.

To know the real error, you must run the actual FA4 kernel on B200 and compare measured wall-clock time against the predictor. No shortcut around that has been taken here.

What I added (Section 3.5)

The predictor computes latency from the six input fields ((b, h, s, d, \text{causal}, p)) as:

[ T_{\text{pred}} = \max(T_{\text{MMA}}, T_{\text{MUFU}}, T_{\text{HBM}}, T_{\text{L2}}, T_{\text{SMEM}}, T_{\text{TMA}}) + T_{\text{launch}} ]

with each term defined:

  • (T_{\text{MMA}} = \frac{F_{\text{MMA}}}{R_{\text{MMA}}(p) \cdot \eta_{\text{occ}} \cdot \eta_{\text{MMA}} \cdot \phi_{\text{fp8}}(p)})
  • (T_{\text{MUFU}} = \frac{E}{R_{\text{MUFU}}(p) \cdot \eta_{\text{occ}} \cdot \eta_{\text{MUFU}}})
  • (T_{\text{HBM}} = \frac{B_{\text{HBM}}}{\beta_{\text{HBM}}(B_{\text{HBM}})})
  • (T_{\text{L2}} = \frac{B_{\text{L2}}}{\beta_{\text{L2}}(B_{\text{L2}})})
  • (T_{\text{SMEM}} = \frac{B_{\text{SMEM}}}{\beta_{\text{SMEM}}^{\text{eff}}})
  • (T_{\text{TMA}} = \frac{B_{\text{TMA}}}{\beta_{\text{TMA}}(B_{\text{TMA}})})

It also explains that (\phi_{\text{fp8}}(p)) is the fp8 boost, (\beta_{\text{SMEM}}^{\text{eff}}) is the SMEM bandwidth scaled by (f_{\text{SMEM}}), the bottleneck label comes from the argmax, and the only inputs are the six configuration fields.

Updated PDF - Same path: .../bundle/paper.pdf - Section numbering shifted: Calibration is now 3.6, NCU profiling is 3.7. - Table 2 Pass column was also widened so the pass names no longer crush the numeric columns.

The paper now closes the gap between "here are the pieces" and "here is the exact latency equation."

docs.text:
User Prompt:

the white box model must include a system architecture of b200 gpu, its execution flow, internal hardware specifics, how do you model them, and prefer to use closed form formula for each part of modeling, for things that are inherently random (like cache miss), model them with probabilistic models. And, before you go, you shall look for online info about how to do hardware (specifically, gpu) runtime modeling to gain some knowledge

Matched Turn: 6

AI:

I did a quick literature sweep and updated the topic intent files to capture the new requirements.

Key things I found online

  • Analytical GPU modeling style: stage-centric decomposition is the norm — e.g., T_exec = max(T_compute, T_memory) + T_overhead (Hong–Kim MWP/CWP). Roofline is a useful diagnostic but papers report >95% error on modern kernels because it misses pipeline stages, cache hierarchy, occupancy, and architecture-specific units.
  • B200 architecture details:
  • Dual-die, 148 SMs, NV-HBI, 192 GB HBM3e, ~8 TB/s HBM.
  • 256 KB Tensor Memory (TMEM) per SM, Tensor Memory Accelerator (TMA), 5th-gen Tensor Cores (tcgen05.mma), 2-CTA cooperative MMA, and a hardware decompression engine.
  • Asymmetric scaling: FP16/BF16 tensor throughput roughly doubles over Hopper, but shared-memory bandwidth (128 B/clock/SM) and the exponential/MUFU unit (16 ops/clock/SM) do not, so bottlenecks shift to SMEM traffic and softmax.
  • FlashAttention-4 on B200: the paper gives explicit closed-form cycle estimates for MMA time, shared-memory traffic, and exponential-unit time on B200, plus the pipeline design (ping-pong tiles, TMEM-resident accumulators, 2-CTA mode). This is a direct source for the formulas you want.
  • Probabilistic cache modeling: the standard approach is probabilistic cache states with LRU update/merge operators, from which you derive hit/miss probabilities per access and propagate them through loops to get mean/variance of execution time.

Sources now linked in the topic overview:

  • FlashAttention-4 paper
  • Microbenchmark-Driven Analytical Performance Modeling Across Modern GPU Architectures
  • Microbenchmarking NVIDIA's Blackwell Architecture
  • Cache Modeling in Probabilistic Execution Time Analysis

Applied to the topic

  • topic-overview.md now requires:
  • A B200 system-architecture and execution-flow section.
  • Kernel decomposition into stages (TMA, MMA, softmax, TMEM traffic, sync, storeback).
  • Closed-form formulas for each stage.
  • Probabilistic sub-models for cache miss and other stochastic effects.

  • Calibration MAPE: 7.37 % on 16 configs

docs.title: What changed
docs.text:
The model no longer treats memory as a single bandwidth term. It traces each FA4 tile block through hardware components step by step:

  1. Q tile load: HBM → L2 → xbar → L1 → RF/SMEM
  2. K tile load: same path
  3. MMA Q@K^T through tensor core
  4. Softmax: MUFU exp + shared-memory row reduction
  5. V tile load
  6. MMA P@V
  7. O tile store: RF/SMEM → L1 → xbar → L2 → HBM

Each hop is costed with sector/line slicing, partition contention, row-buffer locality, bank-level parallelism, and read/write turnaround. Compute is modeled with tensor-core throughput, pipeline latency/II, and MUFU rate. Aggregation is deterministic closed-form (max(compute, visible_memory) + overhead), not an event-driven simulator.

docs.text:
User Prompt:

do analysis

Matched Turn: 11

AI:

Analysis campaign complete.

Root cause of TMA misclassification The three TMA-targeted configs sit in a moderate ratio band: the model predicts memory_time / compute_time of only 1.2-2.4x. NCU SpeedOfLight still labels them compute-bound because the real B200 overlaps async TMA copy traffic with MMA warps. The predictor's pure max(compute, memory) roofline ignores this overlap and therefore over-calls memory dominance.

Slices run - Slice A — Diagnostic ratios: Quantified tma/mma and memory/compute ratios across all 12 saturation configs. - Slice B — Overlap counterfactual: Tested memory_eff = memory_time * (1 - overlap_frac) for overlap_frac ∈ {0.0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75}. - Slice C — Factor sweep: Grid-searched tma_factor × mma_efficiency to check for a realistic single-factor fix.

Key results - overlap_frac = 0.75 raises coarse accuracy from 66.7% → 91.7% and flips all three TMA configs to compute-bound. - The only factor-sweep combination matching 91.7% requires mma_efficiency = 0.5, which is physically unrealistic; overlap is the more grounded explanation. - One HBM config remains misclassified; overlap alone does not fix it.

Proposed concrete revision Replace the max() roofline with an overlap-aware roofline:

python mem_eff = mem_time * (1 - overlap_frac) predicted_stage_time = max(compute_time, mem_eff)

overlap_frac should then be calibrated on a broader matrix and validated against NCU.

What the README says The README describes the tuner as the path for "new compute capability" hardware, where you provide a hw_def header and run microbenchmarks to generate a config (README.md#L252-L272). It does not list Hopper/Blackwell configs among the provided tested-cfgs.

GitHub issue evidence

  • H100 / Hopper: Issue #514 reports that the experimental H100 config segfaults during simulation. The author references #161 and a now-closed h100-test branch, indicating H100 support is incomplete/unstable. Issue #394 was closed after pointing users to A100 configs, confirming no official H100 config is shipped.

  • Blackwell / RTX 5090: Issue #385 shows the tracer failing because cuobjdump rejects sm_120. Issue #488 reports the same unsupported binary version: 120 on an RTX 5070 Ti.

NVBit version Accel-Sim uses NVBit 1.7.6. That release added SM_110 support but states: "direct support for Hopper and newer GPUs is not available; use manual disassembly of cubins instead". So even tracing on Hopper is limited, and SM_120 (Blackwell RTX 50-series) is not supported.

Bottom line There is no plugin to enable Hopper/Blackwell simulation. You would need to: 1. Update the NVBit tracer to a version that supports the target SM, 2. Build or tune a GPGPU-Sim config for Hopper/Blackwell, and 3. Fix simulator-side instruction/opcode support for the new architecture.

I searched the Accel-Sim repository, its GPGPU-Sim 4.x performance-model dependency, GitHub issues/PRs, and NVBit releases for any experimental Blackwell simulation support. Here is what I found.

docs.title: Bottom line
docs.text: There is no experimental Blackwell (sm_100 / sm_120) simulation implementation in Accel-Sim or GPGPU-Sim today. Accel-Sim’s newest supported ISA is Ampere (sm_80/sm_86). Ada (sm_89) is currently proposed in an open PR by mapping Ada binaries to the Ampere opcode table because the two architectures share the same SASS ISA. Blackwell is not present in any branch, PR, or issue resolution.

docs.title: What I checked
docs.text: Source Result Local Accel-Sim clone (accel-sim-framework) No sm100, sm120, Blackwell, GB100, or GB200 strings anywhere. Local GPGPU-Sim clone (gpu-simulator/gpgpu-sim, dev branch, depth=1) Only one hit: a cub header that mentions “SM120 or later.” No simulator code. Accel-Sim ISA definitions (gpu-simulator/ISA_Def/) Kepler, Pascal, Volta, Turing, Ampere opcode tables only. Accel-Sim tested configs Up to SM86_RTX3070 and SM80_A100. GitHub issues in accel-sim/accel-sim-framework #385 and #488 report unsupported binary version: 120 / sm_120 failures on RTX 5090/5070 Ti. GitHub PRs in accel-sim/accel-sim-framework #548 adds sm_89 Ada support by treating Ada as Ampere SASS. No Blackwell PR exists. accel-sim/gpgpu-sim_distribution PRs #80 is a closed/unmerged “Hopper config” PR that only changes hardware parameters (SM count, memory channels, cache sizes), not ISA. NVBit releases 1.7.6 added SM_110 support. 1.8 (April 2026) added alpha TMA support for Hopper and Blackwell. Accel-Sim’s tracer is pinned to NVBit 1.7.6.

docs.title: What this means for our white-box model
docs.text:
Accel-Sim/GPGPU-Sim is still useful as a reference for how to structure a cycle-level GPU model, even though it does not model Blackwell. The key internals we can borrow are:

  1. Shader core as a collection of functional units (src/gpgpu-sim/shader.h)
  2. Configurable counts of SP, DP, SFU, INT, Tensor Core, MEM, and up to 8 “specialized units.”
  3. Each unit is a pipelined_simd_unit with a latency and initiation interval.

  4. This maps cleanly to a white-box model where we track occupancy and issue cycles per unit type.

  5. SASS-to-execution-unit dispatch (gpu-simulator/ISA_Def/ampere_opcode.h, gpu-simulator/trace-driven/trace_driven.cc)

  6. Each SASS mnemonic is mapped to an op_type (SP_OP, DP_OP, INTP_OP, TENSOR_CORE_OP, SPECIALIZED_UNIT_3_OP, LOAD_OP, STORE_OP, etc.).
  7. The trace frontend selects an opcode table based on the binary version (80/86/75/70/…).

  8. For Blackwell we would need a new table, or—like Ada—map sm_120 to Ampere/Hopper if we only care about throughput and ignore new instructions.

  9. Memory hierarchy timing (src/gpgpu-sim/gpu-cache.h, src/gpgpu-sim/dram.h, src/gpgpu-sim/gpu-sim.h)

  10. L1I/L1T/L1C/L1D per SM, configurable line size, sets, associativity, MSHRs.
  11. L2 cache and bank/partition hashing.
  12. DRAM controller with row/bank state machines, FR-FCFS scheduler, timing constraints (RCD, RAS, RP, CCD, RTW, WTR).

  13. This supports the “slice data into N parts and model each transfer through the hierarchy” style you want.

  14. Interconnect between SMs and memory partitions (src/gpgpu-sim/local_interconnect.*, src/gpgpu-sim/icnt_wrapper.*)

  15. Models NoC/ICNT contention, which is essential for capturing bottleneck shifts between compute and memory.

docs.title: Implications for Blackwell
docs.text:
Since Accel-Sim has no Blackwell code, building a Blackwell-specific white-box model requires us to supply the architecture-specific parameters ourselves:

  • ISA additions: Blackwell introduces FP4/FP6 Tensor Core instructions, improved FP8 paths, and 5th-generation Tensor Cores. None of these opcodes exist in Accel-Sim.
  • Hardware parameters: SM count, HBM4 channels/width, L1/L2 sizes, register file size, clock domains, Tensor Core throughput/latency must come from NVIDIA documentation or microbenchmarks.
  • Execution units: We can model new units as additional SPECIALIZED_UNIT_N_OP entries, following Accel-Sim’s pattern.

docs.title: Recommendation
docs.text:
Use Accel-Sim as a structural template for the mini-simulator: - Adopt its separation of shader core, cache hierarchy, DRAM, and interconnect. - Adopt its trace-driven “SASS op → execution unit → latency/throughput” dispatch model. - But do not wait for an upstream Blackwell config; derive Blackwell parameters from the B200 whitepaper and CUDA binary utilities docs, and add the new opcodes to our own opcode-to-unit mapping.

If you want, I can next: 1. Extract the relevant hardware-parameter skeleton from the SM86_RTX3070 and Hopper-config PR files. 2. Draft a minimal Blackwell config/opcode mapping for our model based on published B200 specs. 3. Identify exactly which SASS instructions FlashAttention-4 emits on Blackwell so we know which opcodes/latencies matter most.

docs.title: No upstream Blackwell simulator exists
docs.text:
• Accel-Sim has no validated Blackwell model; open issue #385 confirms sm_120 failures and unsupported binary version: 120.
  • GPGPU-Sim supports TensorCores and SASS traces but has no Blackwell-specific code.
  • No other open-source Blackwell simulator was found.

docs.title: Public Blackwell microarchitecture facts useful for modeling
docs.text:
• SM topology: up to 160 SMs on Blackwell Ultra; 128 unified CUDA cores per SM; four 5th-gen Tensor Cores per SM.
  • Registers / warps: 64K 32-bit registers per SM; max warps = 64 for SM100 (CC 10.0), 48 for SM120 (CC 12.0).
  • Memory: HBM3e on datacenter SKUs, GDDR7 on consumer; L2 cache up to 128 MB on some models.
  • New features: TMEM (Tensor Memory) exposed on SM100 but apparently not on SM120; unified INT32/FP32 cores; new tcgen05-style tensor-core instruction classes and extended mma.sync variants.
  • Formats: FP4 and FP6 tensor formats, plus blockscaled MMA, are supported in CUTLASS.

docs.title: Evidence gaps (must be measured on real hardware)
docs.text:
• Exact SASS opcode encodings for new tensor instructions.
  • Bit-level numeric layout, rounding, and subnormal behavior for FP4/FP6.
  • Per-opcode latency and throughput for tensor ops and TMEM.
  • Precise TMEM bandwidth/latency characteristics.

docs.title: Practical Modeling Path
docs.text:
The research report proposes a prioritized checklist:

  1. Minimum viable: add SM100/SM120 device profiles and update the frontend to accept CUDA 12.8 SASS output.
  2. Functional tensor core: emulate 5th-gen MMA semantics and validate against CUTLASS reference kernels.
  3. Memory & TMEM: add TMEM model with SM100-only enable, plus HBM/GDDR bandwidth profiles.
  4. Scheduler & clusters: model cluster/collective launches and warp-group MMA where applicable.
  5. Calibration: run microbenchmarks and tune per-op latencies/throughput against profiler counters.
  6. Full SASS ISA: implement accurate encodings once disassembly traces are collected.

docs.title: Recommended Next Step
docs.text:
If you want to close the evidence gaps, the most productive move is to generate short SASS kernels on the B200 that exercise: - single tensor-op dependency chains (latency/throughput), - FP4/FP6 GEMMs via CUTLASS blockscaled kernels, - cp.async / TMA / TMEM patterns, and capture cuobjdump/nvdisasm output plus ncu counters.

I can write those microbenchmarks and a data-collection script for the B200 next.

docs.title: New Idea in One Sentence
docs.text: Replace the scalar efficiency factors (tc_efficiency, mufu_efficiency, memory_overlap_factor) with a per-SM execution-unit reservation model that schedules FlashAttention-4's SASS-level micro-ops onto Blackwell's actual pipelines and memory hierarchy.

docs.title: What Changes from the Current Model
docs.text: Current Proposed Coarse phases (load → MMA → softmax → MMA → store) Micro-op DAG (LDG, LDS, HMMA/QMMA/OMMA, MUFU, SHFL, BAR, etc.) Scalar tc_efficiency, mufu_efficiency, memory_overlap_factor Per-SM reservation tables for Tensor Cores, INT/FP units, SFU, MEM Single HBM bandwidth × efficiency Multi-level queueing: Shared → L1 → L2 → HBM, each with measured latency/bandwidth One global overlap factor Critical-path scheduling through the dependency graph

docs.title: Key Mechanism
docs.text:
1. Decompose one FA4 tile iteration into a DAG of micro-ops derived from PTX/SASS.
  2. Map each micro-op to an execution unit and memory level.
  3. Reserve per-SM resources: 4 Tensor Cores, 128 unified INT/FP cores, limited SFU throughput, finite L1/L2 partitions.
  4. Compute kernel runtime as the critical-path finish time, where delays propagate through dependencies and shared resources rather than being absorbed into scalars.

docs.title: Falsifiable Claim
docs.text: A per-SM reservation model using measured Blackwell microbenchmark latencies predicts FA4 runtime with lower validation MAPE than the scalar model, after calibrating no more than five SKU-level knobs.

docs.title: Minimal Experiment
docs.text:
1. Build a Python prototype of the reservation solver.
  2. Extract PTX/SASS instruction counts for FP16/FP8 FA4 on sm_100.
  3. Calibrate five knobs on 8 B200 training configs.
  4. Predict 8 held-out configs and compare MAPE to the existing scalar model.

docs.title: Abandonment Condition
docs.text: If the new model does not improve validation MAPE by at least 2 percentage points, or requires more than 16 measured configs to calibrate, fall back to extending the scalar model with tile-shape-dependent overlap curves.

docs.title: Next Step
docs.text: The proposal is ready for a bounded experiment pass: prototype the reservation solver and validate on B200 measurements. I can start implementing the Python prototype next.

But that label is coarse — NCU reports every profiled configuration as compute-bound, so the main model essentially learns to call everything compute-bound. It does not tell you which unit (Tensor Core, SFU, FP, TMA, TMEM) will saturate.

For naming the specific saturating hardware unit, the execution-flow round models are better:

  • Round 1 (per-SM reservation) is the best trade-off. It reports per-unit utilization and a bottleneck label for every configuration — e.g., Tensor Core-bound for large head-dim-128 tiles, TMA/memory-bound for small or low-precision tiles — with 16.69% validation MAPE.

  • Round 3 (SASS-level RAW dependency) gives the finest-grained answer — which instruction class sits on the critical path — but it is the least reliable for runtime/bottleneck prediction because the small split cannot constrain so many parameters (21.16% validation MAPE).

So: - Use the main refined predictor if you only need a compute/memory NCU-aligned label. - Use Round 1 (or Round 2 if you also care about scheduler partitions) if you need to identify the actual saturating execution unit.

  • Round 1 (per-SM reservation) gives per-unit reservation times for Tensor Cores, SFU, FP/INT, TMA, and TMEM. The unit with the largest reservation is the predicted saturating component. This is the natural bottleneck labeler.

The gap is validation data. NCU SpeedOfLight can give per-unit active-cycle counters (e.g., tensor-core cycles, TMA cycles, SFU cycles). If we collect those for the profiled subset, we can:

  1. Label ground-truth saturated component from the max per-unit NCU counter.
  2. Compare with Round 1’s predicted max reservation time → report component-level bottleneck accuracy.
  3. Use Round 3’s critical-path output to report the predicted blocking instruction/execution path.

So the next move is to turn Round 1 into the bottleneck diagnostic head, validate it against per-unit NCU counters, and use Round 3 to name the blocking path. The main predictor can keep doing runtime prediction while Round 1/Round 3 do the diagnosis.

docs.title: 1. Hypothesis
docs.text: For a carefully chosen set of input shapes, the model’s predicted saturated component and predicted blocking path should match the unit with the highest NCU active-cycle count and the dominant instruction-dependency chain observed by the profiler.

docs.text:
Hold batch and head count fixed (e.g., batch=4, heads=32) and vary the dimensions that move the bottleneck across the critical path:

Target segment Config design Why it stresses that segment tcgen05.mma (Tensor Core) d=128, long s (8192–16384), bf16/fp16, non-causal Maximizes MMA FLOPs per byte; TC issue slots dominate TMA load / memory pipe d=64, long s, fp8, non-causal Low compute per loaded byte; async-copy engine saturates SFU exp softmax d=64, very long s, fp8 Faster MMA makes softmax exponentiation relatively more exposed FP32 scale / update d=128, fp8, causal=False More scale/update work per MMA tile TMA store O small d, long s, write-heavy non-causal Output tile write traffic becomes prominent TMEM read/write small tile / low occupancy Frequent Tensor Memory traffic relative to compute

docs.title: 3. NCU evidence to collect
docs.text:
For each configuration, profile the kernel with --metrics that expose per-unit activity on Blackwell:

  • Tensor Core: sm__pipe_tensor_cycles_active.avg
  • SFU / MUFU: sm__pipe_fma_cycles_active.avg or sm__inst_executed_pipe_sfu.avg
  • FP32/INT32: sm__pipe_fp32_cycles_active.avg
  • TMA: sm__inst_tma_cycles_active.avg (or sm__cycles_active_tma.avg)
  • LD/ST / L1: sm__pipe_lsu_cycles_active.avg
  • HBM/L2 throughput: dram__bytes.sum, lts__t_bytes.sum

Derive the NCU saturated component as the unit with the highest active-cycle count normalized by the number of SMs sampled. Derive the NCU blocking path from the dominant instruction class in the executed instruction mix.

docs.text:

Model output NCU ground truth Accuracy metric predicted_saturated_component = argmax_u T_u Unit with max NCU active cycles Component-level bottleneck accuracy predicted_blocking_path Dominant instruction chain from NCU instruction mix Path-level bottleneck accuracy predicted_runtime_ms Median measured runtime MAPE / within-30%
